chiefComplaints: High-grade fever with chills for 5 days, productive cough with rusty sputum for 4 days, progressive shortness of breath (NYHA Grade III) for 2 days.
hpi: A 62-year-old male, known diabetic and hypertensive, presented to the emergency department with severe respiratory distress, spiking fevers up to 103 F, and productive cough. Chest auscultation revealed bronchial breath sounds and coarse crepitations in the right infrascapular region. ABG showed mild hypoxemia. Patient was admitted to ICU for oxygen therapy and aggressive IV antibiotic management.
relevantClinicalFindings: Temp: 101.5 F, PR: 98/min, BP: 110/70 mmHg, SpO2: 91% on room air (improving to 96% on 3L/min O2 via nasal prongs). Respiratory rate: 24/min. Chest X-Ray: Right lower lobe consolidation. HRCT Chest: Segmental consolidation of the right lower lobe with air bronchograms, suggestive of infective etiology/lobar pneumonia. WBC: 16,800 cells/cumm with neutrophilia (84%). CRP: 84 mg/L.
additionalClinicalNotes: Patient was monitored in the ICU for 2 days (12th Oct to 14th Oct) and then shifted to the Deluxe Single Room (14th Oct to 18th Oct) as standard rooms were occupied. Shifted out of ICU once hemodynamically stable and maintaining SpO2 > 94% on room air. Treated with IV Piptaz, IV Azithromycin, nebulization, and supportive care.
treatmentTakenSoFar: IV Antibiotics (Inj Piptaz 4.5g TID, Inj Clindamycin 600mg BD), Nebulization with Duolin and Budecort, IV fluids, oral antipyretics, and insulin sliding scale.
reasonForHospitalisation: Severe community-acquired pneumonia with hypoxia requiring ICU monitoring, oxygen support, and intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics.
notes: This case tests the TPA billing and room rent audit rules. The patient's policy has a 1% room rent cap on Normal rooms (Rs. 3,000/day). The patient was billed for a Deluxe Room at Rs. 7,000/day. The TPA applied a blanket 57.14% proportional deduction on the entire bill including Pharmacy and Consumables, which is a compliance breach of IRDAI circulars.
realGap: The TPA FHPL applied a 57.14% proportionate deduction across the entire inpatient bill (including non-associate expenses like pharmacy, consumables, and diagnostics) due to the patient staying in a room (Rs. 7,000/day) that exceeded their eligible limit of 1% of Sum Insured (Rs. 3,000/day). Under IRDAI guidelines, pharmacy, consumables, and implants cannot be subjected to proportional room rent deductions.
sourceReasoning: As per IRDAI Guidelines on Standardization of General Terms and Clauses in Health Insurance Policies (Ref: IRDAI/HLT/REG/CIR/195/09/2020), insurers and TPAs are strictly prohibited from applying proportionate room rent deductions to non-associated expenses such as pharmacy, medical devices, implants, and consumables, even if the patient chooses a room category higher than their eligibility.
rawDocumentText:
APEX MULTISPECIALTY HOSPITAL
Plot No. 12, Sector 5, Thane West, Mumbai - 400601
DISCHARGE SUMMARY & BILLING AUDIT SHEET

Patient Name: Ramesh Chandra Joshi  |  Age/Sex: 62 Years / Male
UHID: AMH-2023-99122  |  IP No: IP-10029312
Date of Admission: 12/10/2023 (10:15 AM)  |  Date of Discharge: 18/10/2023 (02:00 PM)
Admitting Consultant: Dr. Milind Kulkarni, MD (Medicine)
Diagnosis: Community-acquired Pneumonia, Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, Essential Hypertension
ICD-10 Code: J18.9

CLINICAL SUMMARY:
Patient presented with high-grade fever with chills, productive cough with rusty sputum, and progressive shortness of breath for 5 days. On admission, patient had tachycardia (PR 98 bpm), tachypnea (RR 24/min), and mild hypoxia (SpO2 91% on room air). Chest auscultation revealed bilateral coarse crepitations in the lower zones, worse on the right side. HRCT chest confirmed segment-specific right lower lobe consolidation. Patient was admitted to ICU for monitoring and initiated on IV antibiotics (Inj. Piptaz 4.5g TID) and oxygen support. He was shifted to the Deluxe Single Room on 14/10/2023 once clinical parameters stabilized. Discharged on oral medications in stable condition.

FINAL HOSPITAL BILL SUMMARY:
1. ICU Room Rent (2 days @ Rs. 10,000/day): Rs. 20,000
2. Deluxe Private Room Rent (4 days @ Rs. 7,000/day): Rs. 28,000
3. Professional Fees (Doctor Visits): Rs. 15,000
4. Nursing Charges: Rs. 12,000
5. Investigations (CT Chest, Labs, Blood Culture): Rs. 30,000
6. Pharmacy & Consumables (IV Medications, Syringes, Nebules): Rs. 65,000
7. Physiotherapy & Nebulization Charges: Rs. 15,000
TOTAL BILL AMOUNT: Rs. 1,85,000

INSURANCE / TPA SHEET:
Policy No: HDF-2023-883921-01  |  Insurer: HDFC ERGO General Insurance  |  TPA: FHPL TPA
Sum Insured: Rs. 3,00,000
Room Rent Eligibility: 1% of SI per day for Normal Room (Rs. 3,000) and 2% for ICU (Rs. 6,000).

TPA CLAIM ASSESSMENT NOTE:
FHPL TPA has issued a partial approval of Rs. 1,06,285. A proportionate deduction of 57.14% (calculated as (7000 - 3000)/7000) has been applied to all bill heads including doctors fees, nursing, diagnostics, and pharmacy/consumables because the patient opted for a Deluxe Room (Rs. 7,000/day) which exceeds the policy room rent limit of Rs. 3,000/day.
simulatedDenialReason: Claim partially approved for Rs. 1,06,285. A proportional room rent deduction of 57.14% has been applied to all associated and non-associated billing items (including pharmacy, diagnostics, and consumables) because the patient availed a Deluxe Private Room at Rs. 7,000 per day against their policy eligibility of Rs. 3,000 per day (1% of Sum Insured).

expectedAppealCitations: IRDAI Guidelines on Standardization of General Terms and Clauses in Health Insurance Policies (Ref: IRDAI/HLT/REG/CIR/195/09/2020) strictly state that non-associated expenses like pharmacy, medical devices, and consumables cannot be subjected to proportionate room rent deductions.
The pharmacy and consumables amount of Rs. 65,000 must be reimbursed in full without applying the 57.14% room rent deduction.
The standard room rent cap of 1% (Rs. 3,000) was exceeded due to medical emergency and non-availability of standard rooms on the date of ICU de-escalation.
